## [00:00 - 03:02]

This is a pubcast shot. I'm John Loomer. You probably don't know these things about attribution. Let's go. So in two prior episodes, I covered view through and click attribution. Now today, let's discuss some of the most confused, if not controversial, aspects of how Metacredits adds for conversions. Now some of these might be obvious to, I'd say, most advertisers, but I guarantee that some are going to be completely new information for the vast majority of listeners. Now first, the most obvious. Attribution counts more than just conversions that immediately follow a click on your ad. So when we envision the typical purchase, this is exactly what we see. Someone clicks your ad, they go to your website, and they immediately convert. Of course, that said, not every transaction is that clean. The default attribution setting is seven-day click and one-day view. So that includes a whole lot of other options. Someone doesn't need to even click on your ad to count a conversion, assuming they convert within a day of seeing it. And the purchase journey can take a customer several days, and across multiple devices before completed to get that credit. So while obvious, these are also some of the primary reasons Ads Manager results will never match up with your third-party reporting. Those URL parameters that you use can be completely worthless in some of these cases. Second, the results aren't necessarily for the thing that you promoted, easy to forget, or mis-entirely. So let's say that you're running an ad for a baseball cap. Someone you pay to reach clicks your ad and they go to your website. But they don't like that hat in your ad. They move around your site and they end up buying a t-shirt. If you're optimizing for the purchase conversion event, the purchase of this t-shirt will be included in the results column. This is again why your Ads Manager results may not match up with your expectations. So let's say for example that Ads Manager is reporting 10 purchases on the ad where you were promoting a hat. But you know that you only sold eight hats. That's not meta making up conversions, like many people assume. It's just that someone who viewed or clicked your ad purchase something else. Third, a single person can perform multiple conversion events. Now keep in mind that meta will deduplicate events if they happen at the same time and appear to represent the same transaction. But there are other options. Let's again assume that someone clicked your ad and went straight to your website. They make a purchase that day. Then they return to your website and make another purchase four days later. Both of these purchases will be counted because they both fall within that seven-day click attribution window.

## [03:02 - 06:06]

You can still isolate the first one if you compare attribution settings and choose this new setting, first attribution. This is one of my favorite features to use because it often lines up my Ads Manager data with the backend data. Fourth, and this is a big one, click attribution includes other clicks. I included this on the last episode and it bears repeating because I went more than a decade without knowing this. I know I'm not the only one. So let's provide the most common scenario where this might come up. You reach someone who's already closely connected to you. Maybe they're on your email list, they follow your social channels or they visit your website regularly. You then reach them either because you're remarketing or because meta prioritizes someone like that when showing your ads. They then provide some sort of click, but it's not on the link to your website. Maybe it's a click to open a video or they add a comment. Or maybe it's even something as minimal as a like. Since they know you, that like could be a reflexive response and they really might have no idea what was in that ad. It made no impression on them. Then within seven days, they perform the action that your ad was optimized for. Let's assume it was a purchase. Again, it doesn't have to be a purchase of the thing that was promoted. They would have bought that thing that day regardless, but the ad still gets credit. Now you can make the argument that your ad contributed just like you can with view through conversions. The difference here though is that view through only allows a one day window to complete that transaction. This type of click gets seven days. And what drives me crazy is you can't separate conversions in ads manager that resulted from clicks on links from those that weren't. It's yet another type of conversion that can't be proven with third party reporting. Now all of these examples are reporting nightmares. If you or a client insist on data and ads manager matching up with some third party tool, you're always going to be disappointed. You'll always assume that meta is inflating results, but that's not really happening. You might be able to prove that the transaction happened, but you have to take Meta's word for some of these results in ads manager that your ad gets credit. So maybe you already knew about all these exceptions to how we assume attribution works.
